name: Risk assessments
description: Falls, nutrition, pressure care, moving & handling
current_process: Care homes conduct multiple risk assessments for each resident, covering falls, nutrition (MUST — Malnutent Universal Screening Tool), pressure care (Waterlow score), moving and handling, choking/swallowing (SALT), continence, skin integrity, behaviour, medication, and environmental hazards. Each assessment uses a standardised scoring tool — for example, the Waterlow assessment scores factors like BMI, skin type, mobility, appetite, and continence to calculate pressure ulcer risk. Assessments are carried out on admission, after any significant change in condition, after an incident (e.g., a fall triggers a falls risk reassessment), and at regular review intervals (typically monthly or quarterly). Each assessment is documented on paper forms or in a basic digital template, generating a risk score and a corresponding care plan with mitigation strategies. A resident with multiple risk factors may have 6–10 separate risk assessments, each requiring individual review and updating. The registered manager or senior carer must ensure all assessments are current, accurate, and reflected in the resident's care plan. Cross-referencing risks — for example, linking a falls assessment with a moving and handling plan and a medication review — is typically done manually, if at all.
time_spent: Estimated 8–15 hours per week for the care team in a 40–60 bed home. An individual risk assessment takes 20–45 minutes to complete (longer for complex residents). On admission, completing the full suite of risk assessments for one resident takes 2–4 hours. Monthly or quarterly reviews across all residents add a substantial rolling workload — a 50-bed home reviewing all risk assessments quarterly means approximately 150–500 individual assessments per quarter. Falls risk reassessments triggered by incidents add unplanned documentation time. The DHSC reports that digital care records enable care plans to be reviewed in 30 minutes instead of 4 hours, indicating the scale of time savings possible.
ai_solution: AI can transform risk assessment from a periodic, paper-based exercise into a continuous, predictive, and interconnected system: (1) Automated scoring — AI pre-populates risk assessment tools (Waterlow, MUST, falls risk) from existing care data (weight records, mobility observations, medication lists, incident history), calculating scores automatically and flagging residents whose risk level has changed. (2) Predictive risk modelling — AI analyses patterns across multiple data sources to predict which residents are at elevated risk of falls, pressure ulcers, or malnutrition before a clinical threshold is reached. NHS England has rolled out Cera's AI tool nationally, which predicts falls risk with 97% accuracy and prevents up to 2,000 falls and hospital admissions daily. (3) Cross-referenced risk plans — AI automatically links related assessments (e.g., a falls assessment triggers review of moving and handling, medication, and environmental risk) ensuring a holistic approach. (4) Automated care plan updates — when a risk score changes, AI drafts updated care plan interventions for staff review. (5) Clinical assessment dashboards — CareCallAI offers 7 integrated clinical assessments (Waterlow, MUST, NEWS2, Falls Risk, Abbey Pain Scale, Barthel Index, SALT) with AI-generated care plans. (6) Trend visualisation showing risk trajectories over time for each resident.
buyer_language: "Every resident has half a dozen risk assessments and they all need reviewing regularly. When someone has a fall, I have to redo the falls assessment, update the care plan, check the moving and handling plan, and review their medications — it takes hours. And trying to spot which residents are deteriorating before something goes wrong? That's nearly impossible when you're buried in paperwork."
outcome_statement: Risk assessments that update themselves from daily care data, predict problems before they happen, and automatically connect falls, nutrition, pressure care, and medication risks into one joined-up picture — so staff focus on prevention, not paperwork.
regulatory_alignment: Maps directly to CQC Quality Statement: 'Involving people to manage risks' under the Safe key question — 'We understand and manage risks, so that care is safe, supportive, and helps people do the things that are important to them.' Also aligns with 'Assessing needs' (Effective), 'Monitoring and improving outcomes' (Effective), and 'Safe environments' (Safe). CQC Regulation 12 (Safe care and treatment) requires providers to assess risks to health and safety during care and treatment. HSE guidance on risk assessment in care settings emphasises proportionate, sensible assessment that does not stifle positive risk-taking.
existing_tech_gap: Current care management platforms (Nourish, Person Centred Software, Log my Care, PASS by everyLIFE) offer digital risk assessment templates and scoring tools, but they function as structured data entry — staff manually input observations and the system calculates scores. PCS and Nourish can generate alerts when assessments are due for review. PASS's PASSgenius reads care logs and flags issues. CareCallAI offers 7 integrated clinical assessments with AI-generated care plans. However, no mainstream UK platform offers predictive risk modelling that anticipates deterioration before clinical thresholds are reached, or automatic cross-referencing of related risk assessments. Cera's AI (rolled out by NHS England) predicts falls with 97% accuracy but operates within domiciliary care rather than residential care homes. AI adds unique value through predictive analytics, automated cross-referencing, and continuous risk monitoring that goes beyond periodic manual assessment.
implementation_complexity: Requires integration — AI risk prediction needs access to care notes, observation data, medication records, and incident logs within the existing care planning system. Homes using comprehensive digital care records (Nourish, PCS) will have the data foundation needed; homes still on paper will need to digitise first. Clinical validation of AI risk models is essential — predictions must be validated against clinical outcomes before being relied upon for care decisions. Process redesign is needed to shift from periodic assessment to continuous AI-monitored risk.
proof_of_concept_availability: Yes. NHS England has rolled out Cera's AI tool nationally, predicting falls risk with 97% accuracy and preventing up to 2,000 falls and hospital admissions daily — a validated, deployed solution at national scale. CareCallAI offers 7 integrated AI-powered clinical assessments for UK care providers. The FinCH (Falls in Care Homes) study provides an evidence base for multifactorial falls risk assessment in care homes. BMC Digital Health published validation of a multifactorial falls-intervention digital application showing reduced fall rates. Multiple working solutions exist, though full integration into a single care home platform with predictive capabilities across all risk types remains an emerging opportunity.
